title: Cross-Tenant / Multi-Tenant Access-Control Testing
summary: The SaaS-specific slice of broken object-level authorization: whether one tenant/organization can reach another tenant's data or actions. Distinct from generic IDOR in that the boundary under test is the TENANT, and the highest-value tests use a two-account (tenant A vs tenant B) setup plus header/claim scoping abuse. Lead with this on any multi-tenant SaaS target.

steps.text: Provision two isolated tenants: attacker in org B, victim in org A. Every finding must be an org-B session touching an org-A object — that separation is the proof.
While logged into org A, catalogue object ids (org id, project/record/file/invoice ids, user ids). These become the org-A targets you replay from org B.
Replay an org-A object id with org-B credentials. A 200 with org-A data is confirmed cross-tenant read.
Test cross-tenant WRITE/DELETE, not just read — isolation is often enforced on GET but forgotten on PATCH/DELETE, and cross-tenant mutation is Critical.
Abuse trusted tenant-scoping inputs: override the tenant via a header, body field, or JWT claim the server trusts instead of deriving from the session.
Sweep the whole app with Burp Autorize: set org-B's session as the low-priv identity, browse as org A; Autorize replays each request as org B and flags 'Authorization bypassed!'.
Enumerate sequential/guessable org or object ids to harvest other tenants at scale; diff status/length.

Harvest ids that leak across the tenant boundary — list endpoints, search, notification emails, exports, referers — then replay them cross-tenant. Don't assume UUIDs are safe; they leak and UUIDv1 is predictable.
GraphQL: global node(id:) lookups and batched queries frequently bypass per-tenant checks REST enforces.

code.cmd:
curl -s https://target/graphql -H 'Authorization: Bearer <TENANT_B_TOKEN>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query":"{ node(id:\"<ORG_A_GID>\"){ ... on Invoice { id total } } }"}'

steps.text: Report: name both tenants, the object, read vs write, and whether cross-tenant. Cross-tenant write/delete = Critical; cross-tenant read of sensitive data = High. See also the generic Insecure Direct Object References entry.

title: Webhook Security Testing (SSRF, Secret Leakage, Signature Bypass, Replay)
summary: User-configurable webhooks are a rich, under-tested SaaS surface. The server makes an outbound request to a URL the user controls, making webhooks a first-class SSRF primitive; the shared signing secret and its verification are also frequently mishandled. Test the outbound side (SSRF/metadata), the secret side (leakage, weak/optional signatures), and delivery (replay).

steps.text: Find every webhook config: integrations, CI/CD notifications, outgoing-webhook/callback URL fields, OAuth-app registration URLs, per-repo/per-project hooks.
SSRF via the webhook URL — point it at an OOB canary to confirm server-side fetch, then at internal/metadata targets.

code.cmd:
# webhook target -> your collaborator/interactsh host, then trigger an event
http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/iam/security-credentials/
http://metadata.google.internal/computeMetadata/v1/  (Metadata-Flavor: Google)

steps.text: Defeat naive host filters with alternate encodings / rebinding.

code.cmd:
http://127.1  http://0x7f000001  http://[::1]  http://2130706433
# or a rebinding host resolving to 169.254.169.254

steps.text: Stand up a listener as the webhook target and read exactly what the server sends — signature header name, and any secret/token leaked in headers or body.

code.cmd: python3 -m http.server 8000   # or: ngrok http 8000 and read the inspector

steps.text: Test signature verification: (a) remove the signature header, (b) send garbage, (c) flip the payload but keep the old signature. Any accepted variant = broken verification / forgeable deliveries.

code.cmd:
curl -s -X POST https://target/webhooks/incoming/<id> \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"event":"payment.succeeded","amount":0}'   # NO signature header

steps.text: Hunt the signing secret: UI pre-filled fields, API responses that echo config, exported settings, verbose errors. A leaked secret lets you forge valid signatures.
Replay a captured signed delivery — no timestamp/nonce check means replaying 'payment.succeeded'/'subscription.active' can grant entitlements or duplicate side effects.
If the app parses/reflects the webhook RESPONSE body, a malicious receiver can inject back into the app (stored XSS, XXE on an XML response, deserialization).
Report with OOB proof (collaborator hit + source IP proving server-side origin), the internal resource reached, and whether signatures/replay protection are enforced.

title: Broken Function-Level Authorization (BFLA / Privileged-Function Access)
summary: OWASP API5:2023. Where BOLA is about objects, BFLA is about ACTIONS: a low-privileged or regular user invoking administrative or higher-role FUNCTIONS (create-user, change-role, delete-tenant, export-all, feature-flags). The gap is an endpoint that checks authentication but not the caller's ROLE. Test by taking privileged calls captured as an admin and replaying them as a normal user, and by guessing admin routes/verbs.

steps.text: Enumerate privileged functions. If you can access an admin/staff account (or docs/JS), catalogue every administrative action and its exact request (method, path, body). These are your BFLA candidates to replay as a low-priv user.
Replay an admin action with a NORMAL user's session. A 200/side-effect is confirmed BFLA.
Swap the HTTP verb. Authz is often enforced per (path, method); a route that blocks GET may allow PUT/DELETE/PATCH. Also try method-override headers.

code.cmd:
curl -s -X DELETE https://target/api/projects/123 -H 'Authorization: Bearer <NORMAL_TOKEN>' -i
curl -s -X POST   https://target/api/projects/123 -H 'X-HTTP-Method-Override: DELETE' -H 'Authorization: Bearer <NORMAL_TOKEN>' -i

steps.text: Guess admin routes even without an admin account. Diff /api/user/* vs /api/admin/*, try common privileged verbs and console paths.

Attack role/scope inputs the server trusts: a role in the body, a claim in the JWT, or a header (X-Role, X-Admin). Downgrade/upgrade and see if the server honors it instead of deriving role server-side.
Mass-assign a privilege field during normal create/update (register or profile-edit) to grant yourself a role — the write-side twin of BFLA.

Automate with Burp Autorize: set the NORMAL user as the low-priv identity, then browse the app as admin. Autorize replays every admin request as the normal user and flags 'Authorization bypassed!' across the whole console at once.
Check GraphQL mutations — admin mutations (deleteOrg, setRole) frequently lack the per-field authz that REST admin routes have.

code.cmd:
curl -s https://target/graphql -H 'Authorization: Bearer <NORMAL_TOKEN>' \
  -H 'Content-Type: application/json' \
  -d '{"query":"mutation{ setUserRole(id:1, role:ADMIN){ id role } }"}'

steps.text: Report: name the privileged function, the low-priv identity used, and the effect (account creation, role change, data export, tenant delete). Admin-function access as a normal user is typically High–Critical. Pairs with the cross-tenant/BOLA entry for full authz coverage.

title: HTTP Host Header Attacks (Reset Poisoning, Routing SSRF, Cache Poisoning)
summary: Many apps trust the Host header (or X-Forwarded-Host) to build absolute URLs, route requests, or key caches. Tampering with it enables password-reset poisoning (reset link points to attacker), web cache poisoning, routing-based SSRF to internal vhosts, and occasional authentication/ACL bypasses. Always test both the real Host header and the X-Forwarded-* family.

steps.text: Baseline: can you change Host and still get a 200? If the app serves normally with an arbitrary Host, it isn't validating it — every test below is in play.
Password-reset poisoning: trigger a reset and override the Host so the emailed link points to your server — capturing the victim's token when they click.
If Host is validated, try the override headers apps commonly trust instead.
Routing-based SSRF: some front-ends route by Host to an internal vhost. Point Host at internal names / metadata and watch for internal responses or timing.
Web cache poisoning via Host / X-Forwarded-Host: if the cache keys on path but the response reflects the header into an absolute URL/script src, poison it for all users.

code.cmd: curl -s https://target/resource.js -H 'X-Forwarded-Host: evil.com' -i   # look for evil.com reflected + a cache HIT

steps.text: Try duplicate/ambiguous Host headers and absolute-URI request lines to bypass naive validation (front-end and back-end may disagree on which Host wins).

code.cmd:
printf 'GET / HTTP/1.1
Host: target
Host: evil.com

'   # send via Burp/nc; observe which Host is used

steps.text: Authentication/ACL bypass: internal-only or admin panels sometimes gate on Host (e.g. Host: internal). Spoof it to reach restricted areas.
Enumerate virtual hosts on the same IP to find hidden apps (staging, admin) that only answer to a specific Host.
Report the concrete impact: a captured reset token, a poisoned cached response served to another client, or an internal resource reached. Reset poisoning and cache poisoning are typically High.
